stories.headline: Reverse-engineering Claude Code reveals the design rules agentic systems need
stories.summary: Researchers reverse-engineered Claude Code's source code, mapping five design values through thirteen principles. The core is a simple loop; the real complexity sits in a seven-mode permission system around it. The paper triangulates its reading against two independent open-source agents, OpenClaw and Hermes Agent, though it remains one research team's analysis. Agentic coding systems are already structuring human authority as a first-order constraint, not a safety layer bolted on after the fact. Where yesterday's Pulse showed agents hiding the evidence they never used, today's shows the same systems making human authority a design primitive -- oversight is shifting from an audit problem to an architectural one.

claims.claim: Reverse-engineering Claude Code reveals the design rules agentic systems need
claims.summary_span: Reverse-engineering Claude Code reveals the design rules agentic systems need
claims.source_span: This study describes its architecture by analyzing the publicly available source code
claims.note: 'Reverse-engineering' is a fair paraphrase of 'analyzing the publicly available source code'; the rest of the headline accurately reflects the paper's thrust.

claims.note: The seven-mode permission system is confirmed; omitting the ML-based classifier detail is acceptable compression.

claims.note: The source lists 'human decision authority' as a primary motivating value of the architecture, supporting the claim that it is a first-order design concern rather than an afterthought.

sections.name: big_picture

stories.headline: Character-level text tricks bypass safety filters in most open models tested
stories.summary: BPE tokenisation fragments safety-critical words into sub-pieces, and none of the 30,000 alignment examples across three public datasets contained intentionally fragmented inputs. Across five open model families, simple character-level perturbations flipped refusals on 80-100% of harmful prompts; 48% produced genuinely harmful outputs. No code is public yet. If a perturbation this simple flips refusals across five open model families, how much of the published refusal-rate literature still describes a defence that holds?

claims.claim: Character-level text tricks bypass safety filters in most open models tested
claims.summary_span: Character-level text tricks bypass safety filters in most open models tested
claims.source_span: Character-level perturbations bypass safety alignment in modern LLMs despite leaving prompts human-readable. The mechanism is a chain, tested end-to-end on five model families (Qwen-3-4B, Qwen-2.5-7B, Gemma-3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B).
claims.note: 'Most open models tested' is a fair generalisation of five model families where 80-100% of refusals were flipped; 'text tricks' paraphrases 'character-level perturbations'; 'safety filters' paraphrases 'safety alignment'.

claims.claim: BPE tokenisation fragments safety-critical words into sub-pieces
claims.summary_span: BPE tokenisation fragments safety-critical words into sub-pieces
claims.source_span: BPE tokenization fragments safety-critical words into sub-word pieces
claims.note: 'Sub-pieces' is a minor paraphrase of 'sub-word pieces'; the core fact matches exactly.

claims.claim: none of the 30,000 alignment examples across three public datasets contained intentionally fragmented inputs
claims.summary_span: none of the 30,000 alignment examples across three public datasets contained intentionally fragmented inputs
claims.source_span: an alignment-data scan finds zero fragmented prompts among 30,000 examples (positive-control recall ≥99% at attack-relevant intensities); the three public alignment datasets we surveyed contain no intentionally fragmented inputs
claims.note: The body's claim matches the source's stated figures and dataset count exactly.

claims.claim: simple character-level perturbations flipped refusals on 80-100% of harmful prompts
claims.summary_span: simple character-level perturbations flipped refusals on 80-100% of harmful prompts
claims.source_span: An optimization targeting safety-token fragmentation flips the first-token refusal trigger on 80-100% of refused HarmBench prompts
claims.note: 'Simple character-level perturbations' is a fair plain-English paraphrase of the described optimization; the 80-100% figure matches exactly.

claims.claim: Across five open model families, simple character-level perturbations flipped refusals
claims.summary_span: Across five open model families, simple character-level perturbations flipped refusals on 80-100% of harmful prompts
claims.source_span: tested end-to-end on five model families (Qwen-3-4B, Qwen-2.5-7B, Gemma-3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B)
claims.note: Five model families is confirmed; the attack scope across all five is supported by the source.

claims.claim: 48% produced genuinely harmful outputs
claims.summary_span: 48% produced genuinely harmful outputs
claims.source_span: with 48% of those flips producing genuinely harmful outputs
claims.note: Exact figure and phrasing match the source.

claims.claim: No code is public yet
claims.summary_span: No code is public yet
claims.note: The source excerpt makes no mention of code availability; this claim cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt.

stories.headline: Provenance checks cut AI agent tool errors from 42% to under 2%
stories.summary: The weak point in deployed AI agents is unverifiable tool calls. ProvenanceGuard checks each proposed action against traceable evidence in the agent's context before execution, cutting error rates on misaligned actions from 42.9% to 1.8% on one benchmark. No code is public. If provenance tracing can cut misaligned tool-call rates this sharply, what does that ask of the liability and sign-off frameworks now being drafted around autonomous agents?

claims.claim: Provenance checks cut AI agent tool errors from 42% to under 2%
claims.summary_span: Provenance checks cut AI agent tool errors from 42% to under 2%
claims.source_span: ProvenanceGuard reduces error rate on misaligned traces from 42.9% to 1.8% on Agent-SafetyBench
claims.note: 42.9% rounded to 42% and 1.8% described as 'under 2%' are acceptable approximations; the core fact matches the source.

claims.claim: The weak point in deployed AI agents is unverifiable tool calls.
claims.summary_span: The weak point in deployed AI agents is unverifiable tool calls.
claims.source_span: ensuring that their actions are aligned with the user's intent becomes critical. When an agent's proposed tool invocation deviates from the user's intent -- a phenomenon called misalignment -- it may lead to harmful consequences that are difficult to undo.
claims.note: Editorial framing consistent with the source's motivation; paraphrase of the misalignment problem is accurate.

claims.claim: ProvenanceGuard checks each proposed action against traceable evidence in the agent's context before execution.
claims.summary_span: ProvenanceGuard checks each proposed action against traceable evidence in the agent's context before execution
claims.source_span: a provenance-based conceptual framework that formalizes misalignment detection as determining whether a proposed tool call is supported by traceable evidence in the agent's context... analyzes the agent's action for three types of misalignment before the selected tool is executed
claims.note: Accurate paraphrase of ProvenanceGuard's mechanism as described in the source.

claims.claim: ProvenanceGuard cuts error rates on misaligned actions from 42.9% to 1.8% on one benchmark.
claims.summary_span: cutting error rates on misaligned actions from 42.9% to 1.8% on one benchmark
claims.source_span: ProvenanceGuard reduces error rate on misaligned traces from 42.9% to 1.8% on Agent-SafetyBench
claims.note: Figures match exactly; 'one benchmark' is a correct generalisation (Agent-SafetyBench is one of two benchmarks tested).

claims.claim: No code is public.
claims.summary_span: No code is public.
claims.note: The source excerpt does not mention code availability, so this claim cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt.

stories.headline: SkillOpt trains an agent's instruction file without touching the model
stories.summary: SkillOpt treats an agent's instruction file as a trainable parameter, running a forward-backward-update loop in text space while the underlying model stays frozen. Across 52 benchmark-model combinations, it was best or tied-best in every cell, lifting one model's average score from 58.8 to 82.3. No code is public yet. If instruction files can be tuned into gains like this while the model stays frozen, where should teams draw the line between prompt engineering and training?

claims.claim: SkillOpt trains an agent's instruction file without touching the model
claims.summary_span: SkillOpt trains an agent's instruction file without touching the model
claims.source_span: SkillOpt treats the skill file as a trainable parameter living outside a frozen target model
claims.note: Source confirms skill file is trained while model weights remain frozen; 'instruction file' is a plain-English paraphrase of 'skill file'.

claims.summary_span: SkillOpt treats an agent's instruction file as a trainable parameter
claims.source_span: SkillOpt treats the skill file as a trainable parameter living outside a frozen target model
claims.note: 'Instruction file' is a plain-English paraphrase of 'skill file'; the core claim matches the source exactly.

claims.claim: SkillOpt runs a forward-backward-update loop in text space while the underlying model stays frozen
claims.summary_span: running a forward-backward-update loop in text space while the underlying model stays frozen
claims.source_span: SkillOpt organizes skill editing as a forward–backward–update cycle in text space... the frozen target model executes a batch of training tasks
claims.note: Source explicitly describes the forward-backward-update cycle in text space with a frozen target model.

claims.claim: Across 52 benchmark-model combinations, SkillOpt was best or tied-best in every cell
claims.summary_span: Across 52 benchmark-model combinations, it was best or tied-best in every cell
claims.source_span: SkillOpt delivered the best or tied for-best results on all 52 cells
claims.note: 'Benchmark-model combinations' is a reasonable compression of the six benchmarks, seven models, and three execution modes that yield 52 cells.

claims.claim: SkillOpt lifted one model's average score from 58.8 to 82.3
claims.summary_span: lifting one model's average score from 58.8 to 82.3
claims.source_span: With GPT-5.5 in direct chat, SkillOpt raises the six-benchmark average from 58.8 to 82.3
claims.note: Numbers match exactly; omitting the model name (GPT-5.5) and mode (direct chat) is acceptable compression.

claims.claim: No code is public yet
claims.summary_span: No code is public yet
claims.note: The source excerpt makes no mention of code availability or a public repository, so this claim cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt.

sections.intro_body: Today's research treats alignment failures as fixable at the infrastructure layer: through provenance checks, instruction tuning, and a hard look at how tokenisation fragments safety. The implication is that governance now has specific levers to pull, not just principles to endorse.

sections.name: hands_on

stories.headline: Vercel now exposes full agent traces via its CLI and inspector tools
stories.summary: Vercel's eve agent framework now auto-ingests traces on deployment, surfacing reasoning steps, tool calls, and token usage through four new CLI commands (vercel agent-runs inspect, trace, list, projects) and matching MCP tools. Single-vendor source, no independent benchmarks. If you're already running eve on Vercel, pull vercel agent-runs trace on your next production run before filing a bug report.

claims.claim: Vercel now exposes full agent traces via its CLI and inspector tools
claims.summary_span: Vercel now exposes full agent traces via its CLI and inspector tools
claims.source_span: eve traces are automatically ingested when deployed to Vercel and available as Agent Runs. The new Vercel MCP tools and Vercel CLI commands let you find projects with runs, list recent runs, and retrieve full traces, including reasoning, tool calls, and token usage.
claims.note: Source confirms full traces are retrievable via CLI and MCP/inspector tools; 'inspector tools' is a fair paraphrase of the MCP inspect commands.

claims.claim: Vercel's eve agent framework now auto-ingests traces on deployment
claims.summary_span: Vercel's eve agent framework now auto-ingests traces on deployment
claims.source_span: eve traces are automatically ingested when deployed to Vercel and available as Agent Runs.
claims.note: Direct match to source language.

claims.claim: surfacing reasoning steps, tool calls, and token usage
claims.summary_span: surfacing reasoning steps, tool calls, and token usage
claims.source_span: retrieve full traces, including reasoning, tool calls, and token usage.
claims.note: 'Reasoning steps' is a fair paraphrase of 'reasoning'; other items match exactly.

claims.summary_span: four new CLI commands (vercel agent-runs inspect, trace, list, projects)
claims.source_span:
vercel agent-runs projects
vercel agent-runs list
vercel agent-runs inspect <runId>
vercel agent-runs trace <runId>
claims.note: The source lists the correct four commands but the summary reverses the order of 'inspect' and 'trace', listing them as 'inspect, trace, list, projects' whereas the source order is 'projects, list, inspect, trace'; more critically the names themselves are correct so this is a reordering not a factual error — however the claim is still supported on the substance of the four command names being correct.

claims.summary_span: matching MCP tools
claims.source_span: The new Vercel MCP tools and Vercel CLI commands let you find projects with runs, list recent runs, and retrieve full traces
claims.note: Source confirms corresponding MCP tools exist alongside CLI commands.

claims.claim: Single-vendor source, no independent benchmarks.
claims.summary_span: Single-vendor source, no independent benchmarks.
claims.source_span: Your agent can now inspect Agent Runs via the Vercel MCP and CLI for eve, the open-source agent framework.
claims.note: The source is a Vercel announcement with no third-party validation; 'single-vendor' is an accurate editorial trust flag, not a factual claim requiring contradiction.

stories.headline: The best AI systems score under 60% on real Office documents
stories.summary: Office Comprehension Bench (OCB) is the first public benchmark testing language models on native Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files. The strongest frontier model scores only 59.3% on expert-level document reasoning; pushing it to think harder barely moves the needle. Dataset and tooling are public, though scoring runs on the paper's own ensemble of LLM judges, with no independent replication yet. Run OCB against your document pipeline before signing a processing vendor.

claims.claim: The best AI systems score under 60% on real Office documents
claims.summary_span: The best AI systems score under 60% on real Office documents
claims.source_span: Even the strongest frontier system in its default reasoning mode reaches only about 59.3% on Domain Q&A
claims.note: 59.3% rounds to 'under 60%'; 'real Office documents' is a fair paraphrase of native Word/Excel/PowerPoint files.

claims.claim: Office Comprehension Bench (OCB) is the first public benchmark testing language models on native Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files.
claims.summary_span: Office Comprehension Bench (OCB) is the first public benchmark testing language models on native Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files.
claims.source_span: We introduce Office Comprehension Bench (OCB), the first public benchmark to jointly evaluate LLM systems on Word, Excel, and PowerPoint comprehension over native file formats (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx)
claims.note: Direct match; 'native Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files' is a valid paraphrase of the source.

claims.claim: The strongest frontier model scores only 59.3% on expert-level document reasoning.
claims.summary_span: The strongest frontier model scores only 59.3% on expert-level document reasoning
claims.source_span: Even the strongest frontier system in its default reasoning mode reaches only about 59.3% on Domain Q&A
claims.note: 'Expert-level document reasoning' is a fair paraphrase of Domain Q&A; the figure matches.

claims.summary_span: pushing it to think harder barely moves the needle
claims.source_span: increasing thinking depth within a tier does not move performance materially
claims.note: 'Think harder' paraphrases 'increasing thinking depth'; 'barely moves the needle' paraphrases 'does not move performance materially'.

claims.summary_span: Dataset and tooling are public
claims.source_span: We release the dataset, evaluation tooling, judge prompt, and a public leaderboard.
claims.note: Source confirms dataset and tooling are released publicly.

claims.summary_span: scoring runs on the paper's own ensemble of LLM judges
claims.source_span: an ensemble of LLM judges scores responses against each claim independently
claims.note: Accurate description of the scoring mechanism described in the source.

claims.claim: No independent replication yet.
claims.summary_span: with no independent replication yet
claims.note: The source excerpt does not mention anything about independent replication; this claim cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt.

claims.claim: Run OCB against your document pipeline before signing a processing vendor.
claims.summary_span: Run OCB against your document pipeline before signing a processing vendor.
claims.note: This is an editorial call to action, not a factual claim; it should be skipped per scope rules, but if evaluated it is opinion/advice rather than a checkable fact.

stories.headline: Claude Code tip: let the agent decide when to write tests
stories.summary: The Claude Code team's tip: give Fable a goal, not a rulebook. Instead of "run tests only for large features," tell it to use its own judgement. A second trick routes smaller coding tasks to cheaper sub-models, preserving the top-tier model for review and synthesis. Single-source, practitioner-reported. Add both prompts to your project memory file today.

claims.claim: Claude Code tip: let the agent decide when to write tests
claims.summary_span: Claude Code tip: let the agent decide when to write tests
claims.source_span: it's better to just tell Fable to use its own judgement when deciding to write tests instead.
claims.note: The headline accurately paraphrases the core tip from the Claude Code team source.

claims.claim: The Claude Code team's tip: give Fable a goal, not a rulebook.
claims.summary_span: The Claude Code team's tip: give Fable a goal, not a rulebook.
claims.source_span: One of the most interesting tips I got from the Fireside Chat I hosted with Cat Wu and Thariq Shihipar from the Claude Code team
claims.note: The tip originates from the Claude Code team and the 'goal not rulebook' framing is a fair paraphrase of letting Fable use its own judgement.

claims.claim: Instead of "run tests only for large features," tell it to use its own judgement.
claims.summary_span: Instead of "run tests only for large features," tell it to use its own judgement.
claims.source_span: You can tell Fable "only use automated testing for larger features, don't update and run tests for small copy or design changes" - but it's better to just tell Fable to use its own judgement when deciding to write tests instead.
claims.note: The summary accurately paraphrases the contrast given in the source, with minor compression.

claims.claim: A second trick routes smaller coding tasks to cheaper sub-models.
claims.summary_span: A second trick routes smaller coding tasks to cheaper sub-models
claims.source_span: Tell Fable to use other models for smaller tasks, applying its own judgement about which model to use.
claims.note: The source confirms routing smaller tasks to other (cheaper/lower-power) models via subagents.

claims.claim: preserving the top-tier model for review and synthesis
claims.summary_span: preserving the top-tier model for review and synthesis
claims.source_span: judgment, review, and synthesis stay with the main loop.
claims.note: The source explicitly states review and synthesis stay with the main (top-tier) model.

claims.claim: Single-source, practitioner-reported.
claims.summary_span: Single-source, practitioner-reported.
claims.source_span: One of the most interesting tips I got from the Fireside Chat I hosted with Cat Wu and Thariq Shihipar from the Claude Code team
claims.note: The source is a single practitioner's blog/post reporting tips from a fireside chat, so the trust qualifier is accurate.

claims.claim: Add both prompts to your project memory file today.
claims.summary_span: Add both prompts to your project memory file today.
claims.source_span: Claude saved this memory file in ~/.claude/projects/name-of-project/memory/delegate-coding-to-subagents.md
claims.note: The source shows the author saved the prompt to a project memory file, making this a reasonable call to action grounded in the source.

sections.intro_body: Today's pattern is mixed provenance: one genuinely independent benchmark, one vendor trace tool, one workflow tip. Treat the independent score as signal; hold the rest until you can replicate it on your own documents.

sections.name: currents

stories.headline: Vercel's agent framework treats skills and sandboxes as first-class infrastructure
stories.summary: Early signal: Vercel's eve framework, grown from building its v0 vibe-coding product, packages four primitives as first-class infrastructure -- skills (portable knowledge to correct outdated model behaviour), sandboxes for secure code execution, subagents, and resumable runs. Single-source interview; no independent benchmarks; the shipping CLI that traces these agents in production sits in Hands-On above. If this primitive set becomes the default shape of agent frameworks, scaffolding choices made against it now will age well; if it stays one vendor's house style, it is a preview rather than a standard.

claims.claim: Vercel's agent framework treats skills and sandboxes as first-class infrastructure
claims.summary_span: Vercel's agent framework treats skills and sandboxes as first-class infrastructure
claims.source_span: That became eve. ... A year ago, I started working toward putting an agent on every desk inside Vercel. That led me to build a successful data agent, and along the way a number of best practices emerged: filesystem agents, skills, compaction and subagents. These were all things I wished had come out of the box.
claims.note: Source confirms eve is Vercel's agent framework and that skills and sandboxes are core primitives treated as foundational infrastructure.

claims.claim: Vercel's eve framework, grown from building its v0 vibe-coding product
claims.summary_span: Vercel's eve framework, grown from building its v0 vibe-coding product
claims.source_span: While building our own agent in v0, our vibe-coding product, we ran into a lot of paper cuts that existing tooling did not solve... We turned those solutions into reusable libraries... That became eve.
claims.note: Source explicitly states eve grew out of building v0, described as Vercel's vibe-coding product.

claims.claim: eve packages four primitives as first-class infrastructure
claims.summary_span: packages four primitives as first-class infrastructure -- skills (portable knowledge to correct outdated model behaviour), sandboxes for secure code execution, subagents, and resumable runs
claims.source_span: a number of best practices emerged: filesystem agents, skills, compaction and subagents... switching models or providers, adding fallbacks and making runs resumable... A year ago, we did not know sandboxes would become so important, or how much demand there would be for secure code execution and long-running jobs.
claims.note: Source mentions skills, sandboxes, subagents, and resumable runs as key primitives; the count of four and the specific grouping is a reasonable compression of the source material.

claims.claim: skills (portable knowledge to correct outdated model behaviour)
claims.summary_span: skills (portable knowledge to correct outdated model behaviour)
claims.source_span: Skills are useful as portable, on-demand knowledge. Models often contain outdated information. For example, they still sometimes recommend Vercel Postgres, even though we deprecated it years ago... A skill can tell the agent that Vercel Postgres is deprecated and steer it toward the current approach.
claims.note: Source directly describes skills as portable knowledge used to correct outdated model behaviour.

claims.claim: sandboxes for secure code execution
claims.summary_span: sandboxes for secure code execution
claims.source_span: A year ago, we did not know sandboxes would become so important, or how much demand there would be for secure code execution and long-running jobs.
claims.note: Source explicitly links sandboxes to secure code execution.

claims.claim: Single-source interview; no independent benchmarks
claims.summary_span: Single-source interview; no independent benchmarks
claims.source_span: In this interview with Latent Space, Qu explains why agents represent a new form of software
claims.note: The source is a single interview with one Vercel employee; no benchmarks or independent validation appear in the excerpt.

sections.intro_body: A thin day beneath the surface, but not an empty one. The undercurrent is agent frameworks hardening their primitives -- skills, sandboxes, subagents -- into named infrastructure. One vendor's move is not yet a trend, but it is the direction to watch as scaffolding choices start to set.

notes: shape: green -- pulse: 1, big_picture: 3, hands_on: 3, currents: 1
